abstract: While discourses surrounding equity, diversity, inclusion, and Indigenization (EDI&I) have become highly visible within large organizations like post-secondary institutions, research continues to document only modest progress toward these stated goals. Obstacles to meaningful change stem largely from the structural nature of racist and sexist domination, which remains deeply embedded in both the formal and informal norms of institutional life. Drawing on a close examination of two university-based EDI&I initiatives and direct experiences of "pushback," this article conceptualizes the phenomenon of institutional gaslighting. This dynamic occurs when universities paradoxically champion EDI&I rhetoric while simultaneously deploying strategies that maintain systemic inequalities and stifle structural reform. By theorizing this tension, we aim to provide a framework for identifying and addressing forms of institutional resistance, offering a pathway to bridge the gap between high-level values and transformative practice within large institutions.
